title: Front-load context in your prompts

body_variants.default: {{micro_short_pct:.0f}}% of your prompts are under 50 characters. Short prompts force Claude to guess, burning tokens on clarification. Include: file path, expected vs actual behavior, and constraints. A specific 100-char prompt saves 5 rounds of back-and-forth.
body_variants.bimodal: {{micro_short_pct:.0f}}% of your prompts are under 50 characters (though your avg is {{avg_len}} chars — a bimodal pattern). Those short prompts are often confirmations or follow-ups that force extra round-trips. Try batching context into fewer, richer prompts.

example_suggestion:
Try instead:
"Fix the login form in src/auth/LoginForm.tsx — it shows a blank screen after submitting valid credentials. The handleSubmit callback should redirect to /dashboard but router.push isn't firing."
fallback_example:
Instead of "fix the bug", try:
"Fix the login form in src/auth/LoginForm.tsx — blank screen after submit. handleSubmit should redirect to /dashboard."

title: Reduce confirmation ping-pong
body_template: {{confirm_pct}}% of your prompts are confirmations (yes, ok, go ahead, etc). This suggests Claude is asking for permission too often. Set up a CLAUDE.md with your conventions so Claude can act autonomously, and use permission mode flags to reduce approval prompts.
example_suggestion:
Eliminate these by adding to CLAUDE.md:
- Auto-fix lint errors without asking
- Run tests after every code change
- Commit with descriptive messages, don't ask for approval
fallback_example:
Add to CLAUDE.md:
- Auto-fix lint errors without asking
- Run tests after every code change
- Commit with descriptive messages, don't ask for approval

title: Reduce debugging cycles
body_template: {{debug_pct}}% of your prompts are debugging. Reduce this by: 1) pasting full error messages + stack traces upfront, 2) asking Claude to add error handling proactively when building, 3) requesting defensive coding patterns like input validation.
example_suggestion:
Level up by including:
- The full error message and stack trace
- What you expected vs what happened
- Steps to reproduce
fallback_example: "Fix the crash in PaymentService.processOrder() — here's the stack trace: [paste]. It fails when the cart has items with quantity > 99."

title: Ask for tests alongside features
body_template: Only {{test_pct}}% of prompts mention testing. Bundling test requests with feature work catches regressions early and forces Claude to think about edge cases during implementation. This is one of Claude's strongest capabilities — use it.
fallback_example: "Implement the user search endpoint and write tests covering: empty query, special characters, pagination boundaries, and a user with no matching results."

title: Use Claude as a thinking partner first
body_template: Only {{q_pct}}% of your prompts are questions. Before diving into implementation, spend 30 seconds asking Claude to explain tradeoffs, review your approach, or suggest architecture. A quick question prevents expensive wrong turns.
fallback_example: "Before I implement caching, walk me through the tradeoffs between Redis and in-memory for our case. We have ~1000 req/min and data changes every 5 minutes. What would you recommend?"

title: Schedule refactoring passes
body_template: Only {{ref_pct}}% of prompts involve refactoring. After features ship, ask Claude to clean up. It excels at mechanical refactoring — extracting shared utils, simplifying complex functions, improving naming, reducing duplication.
fallback_example: "Review src/api/ for duplicated logic across endpoints. Extract shared patterns into middleware or utility functions. Don't change behavior, just clean up the structure."

title: Batch related changes into single prompts
body_template: You average {{avg_msgs:.0f}} messages per session. Try combining related changes: instead of 5 separate prompts for 5 files, list all changes in one. Claude handles multi-file changes well and produces more coherent diffs.
fallback_example: "Rename userService to authService across the codebase: 1) rename the file, 2) update all imports, 3) update tests, 4) update config references."

title: Use CLAUDE.md for persistent context
body_template: Put project conventions, file structure, and recurring instructions in a CLAUDE.md file in your project root. Claude reads it at session start, so you never have to repeat setup instructions. This single file can eliminate dozens of wasted prompts per session.
metric_template: {{total_sessions}} sessions could each save setup prompts
fallback_example:
# CLAUDE.md
- React Native app using Expo + TypeScript strict
- Run tests: npx jest --watchAll=false
- Always use functional components with hooks
- API config in src/config/api.ts
- Don't ask before running tests or fixing lint

title: Use lighter models for routine tasks
body_template: Opus accounts for {{opus_pct}}% of your estimated API cost. For routine tasks like file searches, simple edits, code formatting, and grep operations, Sonnet or Haiku are 5-20x cheaper and just as effective. Reserve Opus for complex reasoning and architecture.
metric_template: Opus: {{opus_pct}}% of spend | Haiku is 19x cheaper per token
fallback_example:
Use Claude Code's model selection:
- /model haiku  → quick lookups, file searches, simple fixes
- /model sonnet → standard coding, refactoring, tests
- /model opus   → complex architecture, debugging hard issues

title: Prefer Explore agents over general-purpose
body_template: You spawned {{gp_count}} general-purpose agents vs {{explore_count}} Explore agents. Explore agents use Haiku (much cheaper) and are optimized for code search, file discovery, and quick lookups. Use general-purpose only when the subagent needs to write code or make complex decisions.
fallback_example:
Claude automatically picks agent types, but you can influence it:
- 'find all files that import UserService' → Explore agent
- 'search for how auth is implemented' → Explore agent
- 'refactor the auth module' → general-purpose agent

title: Let Claude use subagents for parallel work
body_template: You've only spawned {{sa_count}} subagents across {{total_sessions}} sessions. Subagents let Claude search code, explore files, and run tasks in parallel. For complex tasks, explicitly ask Claude to 'search in parallel' or 'explore multiple approaches' to unlock this.
fallback_example: "Find all API endpoints that don't have authentication middleware and search for any tests that cover unauthenticated access — do both searches in parallel."

title: Start fresh sessions more often
body_template: Your sessions triggered {{compaction_count}} context compactions — meaning Claude's context window filled up and had to be summarized. After compaction, Claude loses nuance from earlier in the conversation. Start new sessions when switching tasks or after major milestones.
fallback_example:
Good session boundaries:
- After completing a feature → new session for the next one
- After a successful deploy → new session for bug fixes
- When switching projects → always start fresh

title: Reduce context window bloat from tool output
body_template: {{tool_pct}}% of Claude's output goes to tool results (file reads, command output, search results). This fills the context window fast. Use targeted file reads (specific line ranges), limit grep results, and ask Claude to search for specific patterns rather than reading entire files.
fallback_example:
Instead of: 'read the entire auth module'
Try: 'read the handleLogin function in src/auth/login.ts (around line 45-80)'

Instead of: 'search for all uses of UserContext'
Try: 'find where UserContext.Provider is rendered (should be in App.tsx)'

title: Extended thinking is being used heavily
body_template: Claude used extended thinking {{thinking}} times across your sessions (~{{thinking_per_session:.0f}}/session). This is great for complex problems but uses more tokens. For simple tasks, you can nudge Claude to act directly: 'just do it, no need to overthink this.'
fallback_example:
Thinking is valuable for:
- Debugging complex race conditions
- Designing system architecture
- Multi-file refactoring plans

Skip it for: simple renames, formatting, straightforward edits

title: Explore more MCP integrations
body_template: You're using {{skill_count}} MCP tool(s). Claude Code supports integrations with Linear, GitHub, Sentry, Figma, Slack, and many more. MCP tools let Claude take actions directly in your tools — creating tickets, fetching error reports, reading designs — without leaving the terminal.
fallback_example:
Popular MCP integrations:
- Linear: create/update tickets from code context
- Sentry: fetch error details for debugging
- Figma: read designs for implementation
- GitHub: manage PRs and issues

title: Give Claude a way to verify its work
body_template: You're building {{build_pct}}% of the time but only testing {{test_pct}}%. The single most impactful Claude Code habit: give it a feedback loop. When Claude can run tests after every change, output quality jumps 2-3x. Add test commands to CLAUDE.md so Claude runs them automatically.
metric_template: {{build_pct}}% building | {{test_pct}}% testing | recommended: test every change
fallback_example:
Add to CLAUDE.md:
- After ANY code change, run: npm test -- --related
- After UI changes, run: npx playwright test
- Before committing, run: npm run lint && npm run typecheck

Or use a PostToolUse hook in .claude/settings.json to auto-format/test.

title: Use /permissions instead of clicking allow
body_template: {{default_pm_pct:.0f}}% of your messages are in default permission mode. You're likely clicking 'allow' repeatedly for safe commands. Use /permissions to pre-approve safe commands (git, npm test, lint) and check them into .claude/settings.json to share with your team.
metric_template: {{default_pm_pct:.0f}}% default mode | consider: acceptEdits or custom permissions
fallback_example:
In .claude/settings.json:
{"permissions": {"allow": [
  "Bash(npm test*)", "Bash(npm run lint*)",
  "Bash(git status*)", "Bash(git diff*)",
  "Read", "Glob", "Grep"
]}}

Safer than --dangerously-skip-permissions, shared via git.

title: Use a PostToolUse hook for auto-formatting
body_template: You have {{format_prompt_count}} prompts about formatting/linting. Set up a PostToolUse hook to auto-format code after Claude edits it. Claude generates well-formatted code 90% of the time — the hook handles the last 10% so you never waste prompts on formatting issues.

title: Use background agents for long tasks
body_template: You have {{long_session_count}} sessions over 4 hours. For long-running tasks, ask Claude to verify its work with a background agent when done, or use an AgentStop hook to run validation automatically. This catches drift and regressions in marathon sessions.
fallback_example:
At the end of a long feature task, say:
"Before you finish, run the full test suite and verify all TypeScript types still compile. If anything fails, fix it."

Or add a Stop hook that runs tests when a session ends.
